# Transcrição: 07-Reduce CPM through Auction Insights Analysis

- **Fonte:** 07-Reduce CPM through Auction Insights Analysis
- **Duração:** 11:35
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:00]

I like to use delivery insights to give me an idea of what's happening in the auction and there's three main things that I like to focus on. Number one is keeping an eye on auction overlap. Number two is looking at my ad performance within the ad set and seeing if the ad itself has become saturated, which means the ad has been seen by too many people. And the third part is looking at the competition in the auction and seeing whether, for example, my CPMs are higher just simply because people are outbidding me. So what I would do is, first of all, you need ad sets with enough impressions to show you the data. So here we've got an ad set which in this period, I think in the last 30 days has had 1600 spend applied to it. In here you go into the inspection section of your ad set. Now Facebook do often bounce things around as to how to access the auction insights. Right now you click into your ad set, click on inspects and if you've got enough data it will show here. And then what I'm looking at is, first of all, what Facebook do show is the learning status, learning limited, etc. Now something which I take with a pinch of salt because the learning phase is always kicking in. So there's always a case of, you know, if you launch a new ad set or a new ad, there's a learning phase that kicks in. Sometimes when learning limited kicks in and it says, you know, there's not enough purchases, for example, in the last 30 days that this has been running or roughly there's been 38 purchases, which is not in line with the 50 per week that Facebook are asking for. However, we are seeing an okay performance from this ad set. So you have to kind of take some of this with the knowledge that there's your KPIs and then there's the metrics you keep an eye on. For me, the KPI for this is $40. Now, if I am seeing instability in my results, it could be that I'm not giving Facebook enough conversion data. And that's a common point as well. Because Facebook needs the data to continually refine its model of who your perfect target is. If you're getting the 50 a week, that's enough data. Generally I found that the kind of 30 mark is about right. And it's conversion data and it's what you're optimizing for. So this ad set is optimized for purchase. So therefore Facebook is looking for enough purchases to create the stability in the ad set. If there's one reason that scaling fails when you're pushing really hard on the spends, is that stability of having enough conversions at the early stage? One thing you can do to counter that is when you move from the graduation phase to prospecting is to do that with higher budgets and aim to hit your 50 conversions a week faster. That will set the ad set into a much better condition to scale up because you're giving

## [03:00 - 06:02]

it the best start. That also comes with risk of managing the higher spend and making sure that you're not losing too much money as well. But that's how the auction works. It needs the data. So we can scan across here and we can see what our kind of key metrics look like. And when I'm looking at auction insights, I'm looking at my CPA and against my first time impression ratio and auction overlap. They're the main things that I'm interested in as well as kind of looking at competition because they're the things that have that influence my performance on things that my metrics may not otherwise show. So for example, my first time impression ratio, that is how many people in my audience have seen my ad for the first time. If for example, I'm running a retargeting ad to people that have added to cart in the last three days and I'm spending a huge amount of budget a day and my frequency is super high, then my first time impression ratio would be close to zero because everyone's seeing the ad so many times. Within context, and as we've talked about in the full funnel system, that's okay because when it comes to retargeting, I do want people to see my ad more frequently. I want to keep reminding them and pull them into my funnel. But like with this ad set here, if you're doing it at the top of your funnel and the ad is generating a low number of first time impressions, it means your audience size is too small or you've been running this ad for so long with high budgets that you need to consider replacing out if your main metric is suffering. Again, it's taking into account what your main KPI is and balancing out your KPI versus performance. Here, I can see over this period, it has dropped somewhat right down to about 70% and it's bouncing around there, which is okay for the time being. Then I'm looking at my auction overlap. This is something which within CBO, as long as I have my ad sets within the same CBO, I'm less worried about auction overlap because CBO deals with it. But if I have multiple campaigns targeting audiences that fall into the same ad sets, then that can create auction overlap and can create more instability with my performance as well. At the moment, the 0.24 is not a huge amount, so I'm not too worried about that. Auction overlap of 20%, 25%, maybe 30% that I'm getting more interested in finding out exactly what's going on and trying to solve it. But at 0.24%, that's not big enough to worry. Right now, I'm looking at those metrics. I'm not too concerned by that. I'm looking then at auction competition. What this is telling me is how is my ad performing against other advertisers in the market? If the competition has increased, for example, and I'm looking at my cost per purchase here

## [06:02 - 09:05]

and I see some spikes in my cost per purchase increasing, it could simply be that the competition has increased. For example, it could be someone has gone in with high manual bid. It could be that there's a lot more advertisers now competing for this particular audience. If you think about audiences, the most popular audiences that people target for certain types of e-com could be women 25 to 35. If you're in that niche and let's say there's a big global event that's gone on Mother's Day, I don't know, just something related to your niche but outside of your control, there may be a spike in additional advertisers that are coming with short-term campaigns or someone's going with a really aggressive budget and manual bid that pushes you out of the auctions and that can take an impact on your CPA as well. Here, for example, you can see the auction competition was a lot higher at 31% and my cost per purchase was higher. As that competition dropped, my cost per acquisition, cost per purchase, has also dropped. It could be a coincidence or it could be related to stabilization of my ad set as well. It's just something which I would track over time just to measure and see what's going on. Here, again, we've got our audience saturation. That's giving me an idea of what my first-time ratio looks like and that's related to the frequency as well. Over this period, if everyone's seen my ad on average 1.3 times, it's not a lot. Then auction overlap, you can see here what's going on. There's nothing really of interest. For this ad set, I'm quite happy with the performance of what's going on in terms of how things are looking and how things are progressing. If I have a look at the ad set below it, which has a much higher cost per acquisition right now, I can then see if there's anything in particular which needs optimizing for this ad set. You've just seen for an ad set that's within my $40 CPA target, that's performing okay. This one here, I can now see what's going on. I can see that the cost of purchase has been hovering around quite a high mark here as well. One of the interesting things about this is Facebook are telling me it's still in learning phase. The learning phase is that the data for Facebook is not significant enough to give you hard facts on what's working and what's not. They're still developing in the data itself who your ideal prospect is and stabilizing your performance. Within this, my first time impression ratio is still good at 90%. 90% of people are seeing my ad for the first time. Auction overlap is low. I'm not worried about this ad set from an auction insights point of view, but there definitely is a level of optimization that needs to be had.

## [09:05 - 11:35]

This ad set has been running at $290, it's part of a CBO for $290. It's only got 25 purchases over the last 30 days, for example. That will cause instability. One of the things I would need to look at is whether I increase the budgets for the CBO or maybe put some min spend behind this ad set in particular or whether the ad itself is performing or not or the ad within the ad set. They're the kind of decision points I need to make to determine why this ad set isn't performing in terms of cost per acquisition against the target to try and find those areas of opportunity. Use auction overlap and auction insights to get an idea of where things are sitting for you. If you think about Q4 and busy time in November and Black Friday, often you'll see that the auction competition has really picked up and you'll see a big amount of pressure in the auctions and you'll see your CPMs also going up. But if you've already applied the groundwork on developing your CPM strategies to bring your costs down, to work hard on your conversion rates and keeping engagement high, then it's not just about how much you're spending but how the quality signals are gathered by Facebook as to how you perform in the auction. The other side of it is, and we'll discuss this in the scaling section, is if you are seeing a high level of auction competition, you can push back with higher manual bids. Manual bids are a way of bypassing how Facebook sees you in the auction to try and reach higher quality people. Yes, your costs may go up but also often you're able to increase your conversion rate and your average order values as well. This is an important part of the analysis process. This is something that I'd be checking in on a weekly or twice a month basis to give me an idea of what's happening with my ad sets in particular and whether there's anything outside of my control with auctions or overlap or anything like that, which needs to be monitored. It's a really useful tool to keep an eye on. It's not something necessarily you'd want to be checking every day, it's entirely up to you, but it's something to be aware of as part of your toolkit of optimising your costs and your stability as well.
